Travelers Data Scientists are taking the organization to the next level of intelligence by using advanced data science techniques and artificial intelligence algorithms. This may include the use of advanced artificial intelligence techniques to analyze imagery, text, and other unstructured data. This role will focus on Underwriting Operations within Personal Insurance. The ideal candidate will have in-depth technical experience with data science and artificial intelligence, a proven track record of implementing data science & artificial intelligence solutions into production environments, as well as strong leadership and project management skills. Support various training and skill development initiatives, including delivering training to the analytics community Perform other duties as assigned. What Will Our Ideal Candidate Have? Master's in STEM related field or equivalent. 5 or more years of related experience. Subject matter expertise in modeling/ research/ analytics or actuarial required. Subject matter expertise in value creation and business model concepts. Subject matter expertise in multiple statistical software programs. Ability to develop highly complex models, interpret model results and recommend adjustments. Expertise in advanced statistics underlying data models. Ability to apply emerging statistical procedures to complex work. Subject matter expertise in 3-5 of the following: Regression, Classification, Machine Vision, Natural Language Processing, Deep Learning and Statistical modeling. What is a Must Have? Master's degree in Statistics, Mathematics, Decision Sciences, Actuarial Science or related analytical STEM field plus five years of experience or any suitable and equivalent combination of education and work experience. highlights:
Data Science Careers and DSLDP Internship at Travelers in Hartford, CT - News Usa Today
...
# Data Science Careers and DSLDP Internship at Travelers in Hartford, CT
...
Travelers is recruiting for its Data Science Leadership Development Program (DSLDP) internship in Hartford, Connecticut, seeking students to apply predictive modeling and machine learning to insurance operations. According to the official Travelers Careers portal, the program is designed to bridge the gap between academic data science and corporate leadership, placing interns in a pipeline aimed at long-term career development within the company’s analytics ecosystem.
...
This isn’t just another summer internship. It’s a strategic play for talent in a sector that is currently undergoing a massive digital overhaul. Insurance—historically a world of rigid actuarial tables and legacy systems—is now a battleground for algorithmic precision. When a company like Travelers opens a leadership-track internship, they aren’t just looking for someone who can write Python; they are looking for the next generation of executives who can translate a neural network’s output into a profitable business strategy.
...
The insurance industry is shifting from “detect and repair” to “predict and prevent.” By integrating data science into the core of their leadership pipeline, Travelers is addressing a critical talent shortage in “InsurTech.” The DSLDP focuses on the intersection of technical proficiency and business acumen, ensuring that the people managing the data also understand the risk-management principles that have governed the industry for centuries.
...
For the applicant, the stakes are high. A leadership development program differs from a standard internship because it implies a trajectory. It suggests that the company isn’t just filling a temporary seat for a project, but is vetting candidates for future management roles. In the current labor market, where specialized AI talent is seeing fierce competition from Big Tech, Travelers is positioning Hartford as a hub for high-level analytics.
...
The DSLDP structure targets a specific hybrid professional. Most data science roles are siloed—the “quants” build the model, and the “business side” decides how to use it. Travelers is attempting to collapse that wall. By labeling this a “Leadership Development” program, the company signals that it values communication and strategic thinking as much as coding ability.
...
Candidates are expected to navigate the complexities of a highly regulated industry. Unlike a social media startup, insurance is governed by strict state and federal laws regarding how data can be used to determine premiums. This means a DSLDP intern must understand not only the how of a model but the legal why behind its constraints. This regulatory layer is where many raw data scientists struggle, and where a leadership program provides a necessary safety net of mentorship.
...
However, the structural investment in a formal program—rather than ad-hoc hiring—suggests a more permanent commitment. When a firm creates a named program like the DSLDP, it creates a benchmark for success and a repeatable process for scaling its analytics capabilities. The risk for the company is “brain drain,” where they train interns in the nuances of insurance data only for those individuals to be poached by tech giants offering higher base salaries.

I'm a software engineer and technical leader who has spent 21 years building production systems — and who keeps finding his way back to AI. That thread runs the whole length of my career: an AI concentration in undergrad, a 2017 rotation in our innovation center applying computer vision to underwriting, and most recently a year spent productionizing a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) system and building the LLM evaluation harness around it. Somewhere in there I started a Master of Computer Science in Data Science to formalize what I'd been teaching myself along the way. What I do best sits at the seam where AI systems meet real delivery. I took an inherited RAG prototype to production — the AWS APIs around it, the reporting pipeline feeding business dashboards, and a Model-as-a-Judge evaluation system scoring response precision and accuracy on a weekly cadence. I work hands-on in Python and across the modern stack, and I'm building toward MCP and multi-agent orchestration as my team moves in that direction. Underneath the AI, I'm a systems and architecture person. I started out re-engineering legacy COBOL underwriting logic into Java rules engines that are still running today, and I've been designing customer-facing platforms, enterprise APIs, and microservices ever since. I care a lot about technical writing and documentation — detailed designs, architecture diagrams, the artifacts that actually get a team aligned and moving. Explaining complex systems clearly is, to me, half of engineering leadership. I've led onshore and offshore teams for most of those 21 years — as an engineering manager, system owner, and hands-on tech lead — and I like staying close to the code while helping engineers and new leads grow. These days I'm most energized by applied AI in the P&C insurance space: agentic systems, decision support, and the platform engineering that makes them production-grade. If that's the kind of problem you're working on, I'd be glad to connect.

highlights:
### What is a Data Scientist at Travelers?
...
A **Data Scientist** at **Travelers** plays a pivotal role in transforming one of the world’s largest repositories of insurance data into actionable strategic advantages. Operating at the intersection of advanced analytics, business strategy, and technology, data scientists here do not work in a vacuum. Instead, you will build models that directly influence risk assessment, pricing strategies, claims optimization, and customer retention. The work is highly collaborative, requiring close partnerships with actuaries, underwriters, and product teams to integrate predictive models into core business workflows.
...
At **Travelers**, data science is fundamental to maintaining the company's competitive edge in a rapidly changing market. Whether you are assigned to Personal Insurance, Business Insurance, or Bond & Specialty Insurance, your models will tackle complex, real-world challenges. This includes leveraging telematics data to price auto insurance, utilizing computer vision to assess property risk from aerial imagery, or applying natural language processing to streamline claims processing.
